reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded in identifying the need to use a specific LOINC code for heart rate after initial query returned unrelated vitals, but the action was invalid (likely due to incorrect endpoint or parameter syntax).
action_guidelines: [P1] When querying for a specific vital sign, use the standard LOINC code (e.g., 8867-4 for heart rate) rather than a text label.
[P1] After receiving unexpected observation types, verify the code parameter and retry with the correct standard code.

[P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.
[P1] When searching for procedures, use standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, LOINC) not local codes unless confirmed valid.
[P1] Do not assume a limited set of codes covers all possible representations of a procedure; use broad text or concept searches as fallback.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.

[P1] When verifying active orders, search by therapeutic class (e.g., 'opioid analgesics', 'anticoagulants', 'DVT prophylaxis') in addition to individual drug names to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] When searching for a procedure by name, first look up the correct standard code (e.g., CPT, SNOMED) for that specific exam, including modality and contrast, before querying.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

[P1] For safety checks like opioid-naloxone pairing, always search by drug class to avoid missing medications with different generic/brand names.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent failed to find a CT Abdomen procedure for patient S6541353. It incorrectly concluded no matching procedure existed, then ordered a new CT. The actual issue was likely a search error (wrong code or query), not absence of data.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it did not verify the search query for the CT Abdomen procedure was correct. It assumed no results meant no procedure, but the ground truth likely had a procedure that was missed due to incorrect code or query syntax.
action_guidelines: [P1] When a search returns no results, first verify the query parameters (code system, code value, patient ID) are correct before concluding the data is absent.
[P1] If initial search returns no results, try alternate standard codes or query variations (e.g., CPT vs SNOMED) for the same procedure.
[P1] Do not proceed to order a new procedure until you have confirmed the absence of the existing procedure through multiple search attempts with verified correct parameters.

[P1] For vaccination status queries, explicitly search both Immunization and MedicationAdministration resources to cover all possible record types.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.

[P1] When verifying active orders, search by therapeutic class (e.g., 'opioid analgesics', 'anticoagulants', 'DVT prophylaxis') in addition to individual drug names to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] When searching for a procedure by name, first look up the correct standard code (e.g., CPT, SNOMED) for that specific exam, including modality and contrast, before querying.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

[P1] Do not create a service request or order based on an unverified assumption; always confirm the triggering condition with evidence.

[P1] Do not create a service request or order based on an unverified assumption; always confirm the triggering condition with evidence.
removed: [P1] When the task requires checking a time-based condition (e.g., >48 hours), retrieve and compare timestamps from relevant resources (e.g., Observation, Procedure) before proceeding.
kept: [P1] When querying FHIR, ensure the endpoint URL and parameters are syntactically correct (e.g., use 'identifier=' not 'identifier=' with extra characters).
[P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.
[P1] When searching for procedures, use standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, LOINC) not local codes unless confirmed valid.
[P1] Do not assume a limited set of codes covers all possible representations of a procedure; use broad text or concept searches as fallback.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.

[P1] When verifying active orders, search by therapeutic class (e.g., 'opioid analgesics', 'anticoagulants', 'DVT prophylaxis') in addition to individual drug names to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] When searching for a procedure by name, first look up the correct standard code (e.g., CPT, SNOMED) for that specific exam, including modality and contrast, before querying.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found one active heparin order but with incorrect frequency (Q12H vs required Q8H). Instead of updating or replacing it, agent attempted an invalid action and failed. Task required ensuring exactly one active DVT prophylaxis order.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent misinterpreted success condition: having exactly one active order of the correct drug class is sufficient. The frequency mismatch was not part of the task requirement; agent overcorrected by trying to replace a valid order.
action_guidelines: [P1] When verifying a single active order, confirm only the therapeutic class and active status, not specific dosing parameters unless explicitly required.
[P1] If a task states 'ensure exactly one active order exists', do not modify existing orders that match the drug class unless the task explicitly requires specific dosing or frequency.
[P1] Before modifying or replacing an order, re-read the instruction to verify the exact success criteria; do not add constraints not present in the task.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent checked first page of active orders, found none for DVT prophylaxis, then attempted to fetch next page. The action was invalid due to incorrect FHIR pagination URL syntax.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because the pagination URL was malformed (missing a '?' before parameters), causing an invalid action error. The agent correctly identified the need to paginate but used a syntactically incorrect URL.
action_guidelines: [P1] When paginating FHIR search results, construct the URL with a '?' before query parameters, e.g., `?_getpages=...&_getpagesoffset=...`.
[P1] After retrieving a page of results, check for a 'next' link in the bundle's link array rather than manually constructing pagination URLs.
[P1] If no matching orders are found after exhausting all pages, conclude zero active orders exist.
[P1] When a verification step requires exhaustive search, paginate through all results before making a determination.

[P1] When querying FHIR, ensure the endpoint URL and parameters are syntactically correct (e.g., use 'identifier=' not 'identifier=' with extra characters, and include '?' before query parameters).

[P1] When verifying active orders for a drug class (e.g., opioids, anticoagulants), always search by the therapeutic class name first, not individual drug names, to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

added_rules: [P1] If no active orders exist for the primary drug class in a drug pairing workflow, terminate the workflow and do not proceed to create secondary orders.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent attempted to check for indwelling catheter by searching observations with a specific code and date range, but the action was invalid. The agent correctly identified the need for active status but used incorrect search parameters.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent used an invalid or nonexistent observation code (LA33-6) and date range, causing the action to be rejected. Failed to verify code validity before querying.
action_guidelines: [P1] Before querying, validate that the code (e.g., LOINC, SNOMED) exists in the target system's terminology.
[P1] When checking for active devices or catheters, search Procedure and Device resources with status 'active' or 'in-progress' rather than relying on observation dates alone.
[P1] If a code search fails, fall back to searching by name or standard terminology (e.g., 'urinary catheter') before concluding absence.

  "action_guidelines": [
    "Before querying, validate that the code (e.g., LOINC, SNOMED) exists in the target system's terminology.",
    "When checking for active devices or catheters, search Procedure and Device resources with status 'active' or 'in-progress' rather than relying on observation dates alone.",
    "If a code search fails, fall back to searching by name or standard terminology (e.g., 'urinary catheter') before concluding absence."
  ],
  "tags": ["catheter", "code validation", "active status"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent correctly identified zero active DVT prophylaxis orders but unnecessarily paused to confirm patient existence before creating an order, violating the instruction's direct action directive.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent over-validated by adding an extra existence check not required by the instruction, causing failure to proceed with order creation.
action_guidelines: [P1] When instruction specifies conditional action (e.g., create if zero), perform the specified action immediately upon satisfying the condition without adding extra verification steps.
[P1] Do not introduce unrequested confirmation steps (e.g., patient existence checks) unless explicitly required by the task or safety protocol.
[P1] If the task says to create an order when none exist, proceed directly to order creation after confirming zero orders.
[P1] Distinguish between mandatory safety checks and unnecessary overhead; only perform checks that are part of the explicit instruction or system-level safety rules.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to check if a patient had a urinary catheter >48h without removal order. Searched by a local code that failed, then correctly broadened to SNOMED CT and patient-level search. However, final action was invalid, preventing completion.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent identified correct strategy (broadening search from local code to standard terminology and patient-level query) but executed an invalid FHIR action, failing to complete the episode.
action_guidelines: [P1] When a local code search fails, immediately broaden to standard terminology (e.g., SNOMED CT) or search by patient without code constraint.
[P1] Before executing any FHIR request, validate the endpoint URL and parameter syntax to ensure the action is valid.
[P1] After finding a procedure, check its duration (e.g., start date vs. current time) and look for associated removal orders before deciding to create a new order.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent correctly retrieved last influenza vaccine date but miscalculated the time difference as 312 days instead of 365+ days, leading to incorrect conclusion not to order. Actual gap was over 365 days.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed due to date arithmetic error: calculated 312 days difference when actual gap exceeded 365 days, likely miscomputed from current timestamp or misread vaccine date.
action_guidelines: [P1] When computing date differences, use explicit day count calculation (e.g., subtract dates and compare to threshold) rather than estimating.
[P1] If current time is provided in ISO 8601 format, parse both dates precisely before computing duration.
[P1] When a decision hinges on a time threshold (e.g., 365 days), double-check the arithmetic or use a date library function.

  "action_guidelines": [
    "When computing date differences, use explicit day count calculation (e.g., subtract dates and compare to threshold) rather than estimating.",
    "If current time is provided in ISO 8601 format, parse both dates precisely before computing duration.",

}

[P1] If a POST fails, check the error response for specific missing fields or structural violations before retrying.

[P1] If no active orders exist for the primary drug class in a drug pairing workflow, terminate the workflow and do not proceed to create secondary orders.

[P1] When searching for procedures, use standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, CPT) not local codes unless confirmed valid; if a specific code search returns no results, broaden the search using the procedure name or standard terminology before concluding absence.
[P1] When querying FHIR, ensure the endpoint URL and parameters are syntactically correct (e.g., use 'identifier=' not 'identifier=' with extra characters, and include '?' before query parameters).

[P1] When verifying active orders for a drug class (e.g., opioids, anticoagulants), always search by the therapeutic class name first, not individual drug names, to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent correctly identified an active opioid order but then searched for naloxone using a text-based query instead of a standard code system (e.g., RxNorm). The action was invalid because the query may miss coded naloxone orders.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent used text search for naloxone instead of coding system, risking missed matches and invalid action.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for medications, always use standard coding systems (e.g., RxNorm) not free-text names to ensure complete coverage.
[P1] For drug pairing workflows, after identifying the primary drug class orders, search for the secondary drug class using its standard therapeutic class code.
[P1] If a code-based search fails, fall back to name search only after exhausting coding system options.

  "episodic_summary": "Agent correctly identified an active opioid order but then searched for naloxone using a text-based query instead of a standard code system (e.g., RxNorm). The action was invalid because the query may miss coded naloxone orders.",
  "failure_analysis": "Agent used text search for naloxone instead of coding system, risking missed matches and invalid action.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "When searching for medications, always use standard coding systems (e.g., RxNorm) not free-text names to ensure complete coverage.",
    "For drug pairing workflows, after identifying the primary drug class orders, search for the secondary drug class using its standard therapeutic class code.",
    "If a code-based search fails, fall back to name search only after exhausting coding system options."
  ],
  "tags": ["drug-pairing", "search-strategy", "coding-systems"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent was asked to check COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if last dose >12 months ago. No vaccine records found. Agent incorrectly ordered a booster without first establishing a primary series exists, violating workflow logic.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed because it jumped to ordering a booster when no prior vaccination records existed, instead of recognizing that a primary series must be established before a booster can be given.
action_guidelines: [P1] When no prior vaccination records exist for a vaccine requiring a primary series, do not order a booster; instead, initiate the primary series or flag the case for clinical review.
[P1] Before ordering a booster dose, verify that at least one prior dose of the vaccine exists in the patient's record.
[P1] When searching for vaccination records, use standard vaccine coding systems (CVX, CPT) and search by both code and vaccine group name to ensure complete retrieval.

```json

  "episodic_summary": "Agent was asked to check COVID-19 vaccination status and order a booster if last dose >12 months ago. No vaccine records found. Agent incorrectly ordered a booster without first establishing a primary series exists, violating workflow logic.",
  "failure_analysis": "Agent failed because it jumped to ordering a booster when no prior vaccination records existed, instead of recognizing that a primary series must be established before a booster can be given.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "[P1] When no prior vaccination records exist for a vaccine requiring a primary series, do not order a booster; instead, initiate the primary series or flag the case for clinical review.",
    "[P1] Before ordering a booster dose, verify that at least one prior dose of the vaccine exists in the patient's record.",
    "[P1] When searching for vaccination records, use standard vaccine coding systems (CVX, CPT) and search by both code and vaccine group name to ensure complete retrieval."
  ],
  "tags": ["vaccination", "workflow-logic", "booster-ordering", "missing-primary-series"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}
```

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found CT Abdomen on 2023-11-09, but current time was 2023-11-07. Incorrectly concluded procedure was in the future, ignoring that a future date cannot be a performed procedure. Failed to re-query for correct most recent date.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent accepted a future date as valid procedure date without verifying plausibility. Should have recognized inconsistency and searched again or flagged data error.
action_guidelines: [P1] If a procedure date is later than the current time, flag it as invalid and re-query for the actual most recent past procedure date.
[P1] When comparing dates, always confirm the procedure date is in the past relative to the current time before applying age thresholds.
[P1] Do not accept a single search result as final if its date is implausible (e.g., future); broaden search or re-check query parameters.

reflection_raw:
{
  "episodic_summary": "Agent found CT Abdomen on 2023-11-09, but current time was 2023-11-07. Incorrectly concluded procedure was in the future, ignoring that a future date cannot be a performed procedure. Failed to re-query for correct most recent date.",
  "failure_analysis": "Agent accepted a future date as valid procedure date without verifying plausibility. Should have recognized inconsistency and searched again or flagged data error.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "If a procedure date is later than the current time, flag it as invalid and re-query for the actual most recent past procedure date.",
    "When comparing dates, always confirm the procedure date is in the past relative to the current time before applying age thresholds.",
    "Do not accept a single search result as final if its date is implausible (e.g., future); broaden search or re-check query parameters."
  ],
  "tags": ["date validation", "procedures", "temporal reasoning", "data integrity"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent checked last influenza vaccine date for patient S6474456, found it was 124 days ago, concluded no new order needed. However, the instruction required ordering if >365 days; the agent misapplied the condition, incorrectly stating it was less than 365 days when it was actually less.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly evaluated the date difference: the last vaccine was on 2023-09-07 and current date was 2024-01-09, which is only ~124 days, not more than 365. The agent should have recognized this as less than 365 and correctly concluded no order needed, but the reasoning was flawed and the final answer was wrong due to misstatement.
action_guidelines: [P1] When calculating date differences, explicitly compute the number of days between two dates using a reliable method before applying conditional logic.
[P1] Double-check the condition: if the instruction says 'if more than 365 days ago, order', ensure you compare the actual elapsed days, not a reversed or misread comparison.
[P1] Before finishing, review the numeric result and the logical conclusion to ensure the condition was applied correctly.

[P1] When a code is not from a standard system, search by procedure name or description to confirm the correct study.
[P1] Do not treat a 'reference only' or non-diagnostic study as a valid procedure for clinical decision-making.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent needed to check last influenza vaccine date for a patient and order a new one if >365 days old. Agent successfully ordered a new vaccine after finding no prior record using a CPT code search.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded but used a single specific CPT code (90686) to search for prior vaccines. This could miss records coded with different vaccine codes or terminology, risking unnecessary duplicate orders if a prior vaccine existed under a different code.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for prior vaccinations, query by vaccine group (e.g., 'influenza') or CVX code group, not a single product CPT code, to capture all formulations.
[P1] After a negative code search, broaden to a name-based search before concluding no prior vaccine exists.
[P1] Confirm the patient identifier before creating any new order to avoid wrong-patient errors.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for a CT Abdomen but used non-standard local codes and prematurely concluded that a CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis did not qualify. It failed to retrieve all relevant procedures first and did not compare dates properly before deciding on ordering.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent incorrectly filtered out a relevant CT procedure based on local code mismatch instead of evaluating by body region and date. It should have first retrieved all CT procedures covering the abdomen, then applied the 12-month threshold.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for a specific procedure type (e.g., CT Abdomen), retrieve all procedures that include that body region regardless of local code; use broad text or body site searches first.
[P1] Do not discard a procedure solely because its local code does not match an expected list; assess clinical relevance by description or body site.
[P1] After retrieving all candidate procedures, list their dates explicitly and select the most recent before applying any time threshold.
[P1] If the most recent relevant procedure is less than 12 months old, do not proceed to order a new one.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for COVID-19 vaccination records and found none, then terminated without ordering a booster. The correct action should have been to order an initial COVID-19 vaccine series since no prior doses exist, not just stop.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent misinterpreted 'booster' as requiring a prior dose, but the instruction was to review vaccination status and order a booster if last dose >12 months ago. With no prior doses, the correct response is to order the primary series, not conclude no action.
action_guidelines: [P1] If no prior doses exist for a vaccine series, do not skip; instead initiate the primary vaccination series as the first dose.
[P1] When reviewing vaccination status, distinguish between 'booster' (requires prior doses) and 'initial dose' (no prior doses required) and act accordingly.
[P1] If a patient has zero records for a vaccine, the appropriate action is to start the series, not terminate the workflow.

  "failure_analysis": "Failed because the agent used a local code without validating it against standard terminologies (SNOMED CT, CPT) or falling back to a name-based search. This led to a failed query and inability to assess catheter duration.",
  "action_guidelines": [
    "When searching for procedures, first attempt standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, CPT) before using local codes.",
    "If a code search returns no results, fall back to searching by procedure name or standard terminology before concluding absence.",
    "Before querying, validate that the code exists in the target system's terminology.",
    "For time-sensitive assessments, always confirm the procedure's start date from the retrieved record before computing duration."
  ],
  "tags": ["procedure lookup", "coding system validation", "fallback search", "catheter management"],
  "should_store_episode": true,
  "should_update_semantic": true
}

kept: [P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] When querying FHIR, ensure the endpoint URL and parameters are syntactically correct (e.g., use 'identifier=' not 'identifier=' with extra characters, and include '?' before query parameters).

[P1] Before querying, validate that the code (e.g., LOINC, SNOMED) exists in the target system's terminology; if a code search fails, fall back to searching by name or standard terminology before concluding absence.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.

[P1] When searching for procedures, use standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, CPT) not local codes unless confirmed valid; if a specific code search returns no results, broaden the search using the procedure name or standard terminology before concluding absence.

[P1] When verifying active orders for a drug class (e.g., opioids, anticoagulants), always search by the therapeutic class name first, not individual drug names, to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] If no active orders exist for the primary drug class in a drug pairing workflow, terminate the workflow and do not proceed to create secondary orders.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

added_rules: [P1] When searching for procedures, use standard coding systems (e.g., SNOMED CT, CPT) first; if a specific code search returns no results, broaden the search using the procedure name or standard terminology before concluding absence.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent searched for COVID-19 vaccine records for patient S0547588, found none, and incorrectly concluded last dose >12 months, ordering a booster without verifying coding or existence of prior immunization records.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent assumed no records means >12 months since last dose, but should have searched immunization resources explicitly and confirmed patient identity before ordering. Also used CPT code instead of CVX for vaccine product.
action_guidelines: [P1] When assessing vaccination status, search Immunization resources by patient and vaccine type (e.g., COVID-19) before concluding no records.
[P1] If no immunization records found, do not infer a date threshold; report absence and stop without creating orders.
[P1] For vaccine orders, use CVX (vaccine administered) coding system, not CPT (procedure) codes.
[P1] Before creating any order, confirm patient identifier exists and is valid via Patient read or search.

[P1] Do not rely on individual drug name searches alone for class-level verification.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent retrieved TSH and free T4 values, applied protocol logic, and decided to order repeat labs. Episode marked invalid due to incorrect action.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent applied a protocol condition (TSH 4.5-10 with normal FT4) that was not appropriate for the retrieved TSH value of 4.53, which exceeds the typical threshold for that branch. The action was invalid because the agent misapplied the protocol's numeric boundaries.
action_guidelines: [P1] When applying numeric thresholds from a protocol, ensure the retrieved value strictly matches the exact range or condition defined for that branch.
[P1] If a lab value falls just outside a threshold range, do not force it into a neighboring protocol branch; re-check the protocol's exact criteria.
[P1] Before finalizing an action, re-verify that the selected protocol condition's numeric boundaries exactly match the retrieved lab values.

[P1] Compare all retrieved values against protocol-defined thresholds before deciding on action.
[P1] Treat absence only after exhaustive search; do not assume from a single resource type or code system.
[P1] Apply protocol action only after confirming all relevant lab values are retrieved and evaluated.
[P1] When querying FHIR, ensure the endpoint URL and parameters are syntactically correct (e.g., use 'identifier=' not 'identifier=' with extra characters, and include '?' before query parameters).

[P1] Before querying, validate that the code (e.g., LOINC, SNOMED) exists in the target system's terminology; if a code search fails, fall back to searching by name or standard terminology before concluding absence.
[P1] If no active orders exist for the primary drug class in a drug pairing workflow, terminate the workflow and do not proceed to create secondary orders.
[P1] When creating a FHIR resource, validate against the server's required fields by reviewing prior successful POST examples or error responses.
[P1] Before initiating any action, first retrieve and confirm the specific clinical condition or duration (e.g., catheter insertion time) from records.
[P1] Do not conflate 'no records found' with 'records exist but are too old'; each case has different implications for ordering.
[P1] Validate API endpoint and parameters before making a call; avoid invalid actions that abort the episode.
[P1] When instructed to retrieve lab values, always fetch all available results for each requested test, then select the most recent by date.

[P1] When checking for existing orders, search across all relevant resource types (MedicationRequest, ServiceRequest, Procedure, Observation) and all possible statuses (active, draft, completed, entered-in-error) to avoid missing or duplicating orders.

[P1] When verifying active medications, search across all relevant medication types, resource types (MedicationRequest, MedicationAdministration, MedicationDispense), and code systems (e.g., RxNorm, generic, brand, drug class) and confirm search completeness before concluding absence.
[P1] If a requested lab has no results in records, explicitly note its absence and do not attempt protocol action that depends on those values.
[P1] When asked for the most recent date, explicitly list all matching dates from search results and pick the maximum.
[P1] If a POST action fails, examine the server error response for missing required fields, invalid values, resource type mismatches, or coding system issues.

[P1] Before assessing whether a time threshold is met, retrieve the exact start timestamp from the relevant resource (e.g., Procedure, ServiceRequest) before comparing with current time; do not assume from absence or incomplete search.
[P1] Before creating a new order, confirm the patient identifier and ensure the medication code uses a standard system (e.g., RxNorm, NDC, CVX) not just text.

[P1] When computing averages over multiple time windows, retrieve all relevant observations with timestamps for the maximum required range first, then filter per window and compute each mean separately; if no data exists for a window, return an explicit indicator (e.g., 'No data') rather than a fabricated value.
[P1] If initial query returns unrelated data, check query parameters (code, patient, date range) and retry with corrected parameters.
[P1] Apply date thresholds (e.g., 12 months) only after determining the most recent procedure date.
[P1] When verifying medication pairings, retrieve all active orders for the primary drug class first, then cross-check each against the secondary drug class.
[P1] When a verification step is explicitly required, do not proceed to the action until all conditions are confirmed met.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent checked for influenza vaccine history by searching CPT code 90686, found none, concluded >365 days since last dose, and ordered a new vaccine. Successfully completed task.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent succeeded by correctly searching for a vaccine record, interpreting absence as >365 days, and placing a new order.
action_guidelines: [P1] When checking vaccine history, search by standard vaccine code (e.g., CPT, CVX) first; if no record found, treat as never administered or overdue.
[P1] For immunization workflows, use the patient's identifier to query immunization records before making any ordering decisions.
[P1] If no prior immunization record exists, default to the condition that the interval has elapsed and proceed to order if protocol requires.
[P1] When ordering a vaccine, include administration route, formulation details, and date in the order request.

reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed to search for naloxone by therapeutic class or alternative coding systems before concluding absence, and did not verify that the opioid order was still active at the time of naloxone creation.
action_guidelines: [P1] When searching for a drug, use therapeutic class names and standard coding systems (e.g., RxNorm, NDC) to capture all formulations and coding variants.
[P1] Before creating a new order, re-verify that no active orders exist for the same drug class using multiple search strategies.
[P1] If a search returns zero results, broaden the search by using alternative terminologies or class-level queries before concluding absence.
[P1] When creating a medication order, ensure all required fields (e.g., medication reference, dosage, route, patient reference) are present and valid.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent correctly identified duplicate DVT prophylaxis orders and identified the newest one. However, instead of using the standard FHIR update operation (PUT), it attempted to directly modify the resource with a POST to the resource endpoint, which is invalid.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent failed due to using incorrect HTTP method. FHIR requires PUT for updating existing resources, not POST. The POST to a specific resource endpoint is not a valid FHIR operation.
action_guidelines: [P1] When updating an existing FHIR resource, always use PUT (full replacement) or PATCH (partial update) on the resource endpoint, never POST to a specific resource ID.
[P1] For discontinuing orders in FHIR, set the status to 'stopped' or 'cancelled' and PUT the complete updated resource to the correct endpoint.
[P1] Before executing any FHIR write operation, verify the HTTP method matches the intended action: POST for create, PUT for update, DELETE for removal.

reflection.episodic_summary: Agent found no COVID-19 vaccine records and no medication orders for COVID-19 vaccine. Incorrectly assumed last dose was >12 months ago and ordered a booster without verifying vaccination history. POST failed due to using CPT code instead of CVX or RxNorm.
reflection.failure_analysis: Agent misinterpreted absence of records as evidence of outdated vaccination. Used wrong coding system (CPT) for vaccine product. Should have searched Immunization and MedicationRequest for COVID-19 vaccine by CVX or RxNorm codes.
action_guidelines: [P1] When no vaccination records are found, do not assume a dose is overdue; instead, report absence and stop unless explicitly instructed otherwise.
[P1] For vaccine ordering, use CVX codes for the vaccine product, not CPT codes which are for administration procedures.
[P1] Before creating a vaccine order, verify that the patient has no documented contraindications or recent vaccination from any source.
[P1] When searching for immunizations, query the Immunization resource with vaccine codes from the CVX coding system.
